name: Parthians
shortDescription: The Parthians were not a deity, but a powerful Iranian people who posed a significant military threat to the Roman Empire for centuries. They are included here because they were often invoked in Roman religious and political discourse concerning war and victory.
description: The Parthians were not a god or goddess in Roman mythology. They were a real, historical people - a major Iranian power that existed from the 3rd century BC to the 3rd century AD. Originating from Parthia (modern-day northeastern Iran), they established an empire that rivaled and frequently clashed with the Roman Republic and Empire. Their military prowess, particularly their cataphracts (heavily armored cavalry) and horse archers, made them a formidable opponent. Roman emperors and generals often invoked the threat of the Parthians to justify military campaigns, political actions, and religious observances. Victory over the Parthians, or even the avoidance of defeat, was seen as a sign of divine favor. Therefore, while not a deity, the 'Parthians' functioned as a kind of negative force or anti-deity in the Roman mindset, representing a constant challenge to Roman dominance and a test of Mars' (the god of war) favor. References to the Parthians are found in Roman historical accounts, poetry, and even in some religious contexts, albeit indirectly, as a challenge to Roman power and the efficacy of Roman gods, particularly Mars Gradivus (the marching god of war) and Mars Ultor (the avenger). Their constant threat shaped Roman military strategy, political propaganda, and even religious practices related to war and victory. The desire to avenge defeats suffered at the hands of the Parthians fueled many Roman military expeditions into the East.
